title: Isaiah 32
subtitle: The King Who Reigns in Righteousness

sections.header: Verses 1–8 — “Each One Will Be Like a Shelter from the Wind”

heb.paragraph: "See, a king will reign in righteousness (ṣedeq), and rulers will rule with justice" (v.1). The messianic king: each leader will be a shelter from the wind, a refuge from the storm, streams of water in the desert, the shadow of a great rock in a thirsty land (v.2). Eyes will see, ears will hear, the stammering tongue will speak fluently (vv.3–4). Fools will no longer be called noble, nor scoundrels respected (v.5). The social order is corrected: the fool’s folly is exposed, the noble’s nobility is genuine (vv.5–8).

notes.note: "A king will reign in righteousness. Each one will be like a shelter from the wind, streams of water in the desert, the shadow of a great rock." Four images of the messianic leader: shelter, refuge, water, shadow. Protection, provision, rest.
"The eyes of those who see will no longer be closed. The mind of the rash will know and understand." The reversals of ch.6’s hardening commission. Seeing restored, hearing opened, stammering cured.

sections.header: Verses 9–20 — “Till the Spirit Is Poured on Us from on High”

heb.paragraph: "Till the Spirit (rūaḥ) is poured upon us from on high, and the desert becomes a fertile field, and the fertile field seems like a forest" (v.15). The complacent women are warned: within a year the harvest will fail (vv.9–14). The land will be overgrown with thorns (v.13). The palace abandoned, the city forsaken (v.14). BUT: until the Spirit is poured. The Spirit-outpouring transforms desert to garden (v.15). Justice dwells in the desert; righteousness in the fertile field (v.16). The fruit of righteousness: peace. The effect of righteousness: quietness and confidence forever (v.17). Peter quotes Joel’s Spirit-outpouring at Pentecost, but Isaiah 32:15 is the older promise.

notes.note: "Till the Spirit is poured upon us from on high, and the desert becomes a fertile field." The Spirit as water: poured, transforming, greening. The Pentecost promise in Isaiah.

"The fruit of that righteousness will be peace; its effect will be quietness and confidence forever." The equation: righteousness → peace → quietness → confidence. Each produces the next. The same vocabulary as 30:15.

ppl.name: king
ppl.text: king is a key figure in The King Who Reigns in Righteousness.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVFor fools speak folly, their hearts are bent on evil: They practice ungodliness and spread error concerning the Lord; the hungry they leave empty and from the thirsty they withhold water.ESVFor the fool speaks folly, and his heart is busy with iniquity, to practice ungodliness, to utter error concerning the LORD, to leave the craving of the hungry unsatisfied, and to deprive the thirsty of drink.NIVThe fearful heart will know and understand, and the stammering tongue will be fluent and clear.ESVThe heart of the hasty will understand and know, and the tongue of the stammerers will hasten to speak distinctly.

rows.label: Spirit Poured Out — 32:9–20
rows.text: "Till the SPIRIT IS POURED from on high." Desert → garden. "The fruit of righteousness: PEACE, QUIETNESS, CONFIDENCE forever."

hebtext.note: "See, a king will reign in righteousness (ṣedeq), and rulers will rule with justice" (v.1). The messianic king: each leader will be a shelter from the wind, a refuge from the storm, streams of water in the desert, the shadow of a great rock in a thirsty land (v.2). Eyes will see, ears will hear, the stammering tongue will speak fluently (vv.3–4). Fools will no longer be called noble, nor scoundrels respected (v.5). The social order is corrected: the fool’s folly is exposed, the noble’s nobility is genuine (vv.5–8).

"Till the Spirit (rūaḥ) is poured upon us from on high, and the desert becomes a fertile field, and the fertile field seems like a forest" (v.15). The complacent women are warned: within a year the harvest will fail (vv.9–14). The land will be overgrown with thorns (v.13). The palace abandoned, the city forsaken (v.14). BUT: until the Spirit is poured. The Spirit-outpouring transforms desert to garden (v.15). Justice dwells in the desert; righteousness in the fertile field (v.16). The fruit of righteousness: peace. The effect of righteousness: quietness and confidence forever (v.17). Peter quotes Joel’s Spirit-outpouring at Pentecost, but Isaiah 32:15 is the older promise.

themes.note: Justice and Prophecy peak: the righteous king + the Spirit-outpouring. Peace as the fruit of righteousness. The equation from 30:15 fulfilled: rest, quietness, confidence.
